title: Job Interview
setting: A modern office building in Downtown Beirut (Solidere). You have an interview at a marketing company. You enter the lobby, check in with the receptionist, and are led to a meeting room where the hiring manager is waiting.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: وِصِلْتْ عَالمَكْتَب بْوَسَط بَيروت. المَبْنى حِلْو وحَديس. فِتِّتْ عَاللّوبي ورْحِتْ عَالاِسْتِقْبال.
branches.transliteration: Wṣilt ʕal-maktab b-wasaṭ Bayrūt. Il-mabna ḥilw w ḥadīs. Fittit ʕal-lobby w rḥit ʕal-istiʔbēl.
branches.english: You arrive at the office in Downtown Beirut. The building is nice and modern. You enter the lobby and go to the reception.

branches.speaker: Receptionist
branches.arabic: أَهْلاً! كيف فيني ساعْدَك؟
branches.transliteration: Ahlan! Kīf fīnī sēʕdak?
branches.english: Hello! How can I help you?

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose how to introduce yourself:

choices.text: I have an interview appointment at 10
choices.textArabic: عِنْدي مَوْعِد مُقابْلة السّاعة عَشْرة
choices.textTransliteration: ʕIndī mawʕid muʔāble is-sēʕa ʕashra

choices.text: I'm here for a job interview with Mr. Haddad
choices.textArabic: أَنا هَون لَمُقابْلة شُغُل مَع السَيِّد حَدّاد
choices.textTransliteration: Ana hōn la-muʔāble shughul maʕ is-sayyid Ḥaddād
choices.culturalNote: Using 'is-sayyid' (السيّد) is the formal way to address someone in a professional setting. In casual Lebanese, people often just use the first name.

branches.speaker: Receptionist
branches.arabic: أَهْلاً وسَهْلاً. شو إسْمَك الكامِل؟ خَلّيني شَيِّك بِالsysteme.
branches.transliteration: Ahla w sahla. Shū ismak il-kēmil? Khallīnī shayyik bil-systeme.
branches.english: Welcome. What is your full name? Let me check the system.
branches.culturalTip: Code-switching between Arabic and French/English is extremely common in Lebanese professional settings. Words like 'systeme', 'rendez-vous', and 'interview' are used naturally in everyday conversation.

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: إسْمي [إسْمَك]. عِنْدي rendez-vous مَع قِسِم الـmarketing.
branches.transliteration: Ismī [ismak]. ʕIndī rendez-vous maʕ ʔisim il-marketing.
branches.english: My name is [your name]. I have an appointment with the marketing department.

branches.speaker: Receptionist
branches.arabic: تَمام، لَقَيتَك عَالنِّظام. تفَضَّل اقْعُد هَون، السَيِّد حَدّاد رَح يِجي يوخْدَك بَعْد دَقايِق.
branches.transliteration: Tamēm, laʔaytak ʕan-niẓām. Tfaḍḍal iʔʕud hōn, is-sayyid Ḥaddād raḥ yijī yēkhdak baʕd daʔāyiʔ.
branches.english: Perfect, I found you in the system. Please sit here, Mr. Haddad will come get you in a few minutes.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: بَعْد خَمْس دَقايِق، إِجا السَيِّد حَدّاد. رِجّال بِالأَرْبَعينات، لابِس costume رَسْمي.
branches.transliteration: Baʕd khams daʔāyiʔ, ija is-sayyid Ḥaddād. Rijjēl bil-arbaʕīnēt, lēbis costume rasmī.
branches.english: After five minutes, Mr. Haddad arrives. A man in his forties, wearing a formal suit.

branches.speaker: Mr. Haddad
branches.arabic: أَهْلا وسَهْلاً فيك! أَنا نَبيل حَدّاد، مُدير قِسِم الـmarketing. تفَضَّل مَعي عَالمَكْتَب.
branches.transliteration: Ahla w sahla fīk! Ana Nabīl Ḥaddād, mudīr ʔisim il-marketing. Tfaḍḍal maʕī ʕal-maktab.
branches.english: Welcome! I am Nabeel Haddad, marketing department manager. Please come with me to the office.
branches.culturalTip: In Lebanese interviews, the conversation often starts informally before getting into specifics. Building rapport and personal connection matters as much as qualifications.

branches.speaker: Mr. Haddad
branches.arabic: خَبِّرني عَن حالَك. شو خِبْرِتَك بْمَجال الـmarketing؟ وين كِنْت تِشْتِغِل قَبْل؟
branches.transliteration: Khabbirnī ʕan ḥēlak. Shū khibritak b-majēl il-marketing? Wayn kint tishtighil ʔabl?
branches.english: Tell me about yourself. What is your experience in marketing? Where were you working before?

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose how to describe your experience:

choices.text: I have 3 years of experience in digital marketing
choices.textArabic: عِنْدي تْلات سْنين خِبْرة بِالـdigital marketing. كِنْت إشْتِغِل بْشَرِكة بَيروت لِلإعْلانات
choices.textTransliteration: ʕIndī tlēt snīn khibra bil-digital marketing. Kint ishtighil b-sharikit Bayrūt lil-iʕlēnēt

choices.text: I just graduated but I did internships
choices.textArabic: لِسّاتْني مِتْخَرِّج بَس عْمِلْت كَذا stage بْشَرِكات كْبيرة
choices.textTransliteration: Lissētnī mitkharrij bass ʕmilt kaza stage b-sharikēt kbīre
choices.culturalNote: The French word 'stage' (internship) is universally used in Lebanese professional Arabic instead of the MSA word 'tadrīb'.

branches.speaker: Mr. Haddad
branches.arabic: كْتير مْنيح. وشو المَهارات يَلّي عِنْدَك؟ بْتِعْرِف تِشْتِغِل عَالـsocial media وcontent creation؟
branches.transliteration: Ktīr mnīḥ. W shū il-mahērēt yallī ʕandak? Btiʕrif tishtighil ʕal-social media w content creation?
branches.english: Very good. And what skills do you have? Do you know how to work on social media and content creation?

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: إي طَبْعاً. بَعْرِف كَمان أَعْمِل campaigns وأَحَلِّل الـdata. وبِحْكي عَرَبي وإنْكليزي وفْرَنْساوي.
branches.transliteration: Ē ṭabʕan. Baʕrif kamēn aʕmil campaigns w aḥallil il-data. W biḥkī ʕarabī w inglīzī w fransēwī.
branches.english: Yes of course. I also know how to run campaigns and analyze data. And I speak Arabic, English, and French.
branches.culturalTip: Trilingual ability (Arabic, French, English) is a major asset in the Lebanese job market and is often expected for professional positions, especially in marketing and business.

branches.speaker: Mr. Haddad
branches.arabic: حِلْو كْتير. هَلَّق خَلّيني إسْأَلَك عَن تَوَقُّعاتَك لِلمَعاش. شو الـsalaire يَلّي عَم تِتْوَقَّعو؟
branches.transliteration: Ḥilw ktīr. Hallaʔ khallīnī isʔalak ʕan tawaʔʔuʕētak lil-maʕēsh. Shū il-salaire yallī ʕam titwaʔʔaʕo?
branches.english: Very nice. Now let me ask you about your salary expectations. What salary are you expecting?

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose how to handle the salary question:

choices.text: State a specific number confidently
choices.textArabic: بْحَسَب خِبْرِتي، عَم تْوَقَّع حَوالي أَلْف وخَمْسمية دولار فْرِش بِالشَّهِر
choices.textTransliteration: B-ḥasab khibritī, ʕam twaʔʔaʕ ḥawēlī alf w khamsumiyye dolēr fresh bish-shahar

choices.text: Ask about their range first
choices.textArabic: بْفَضِّل أَعْرِف الـrange يَلّي عِنْدْكُن لَهالوَظيفة أَوَّل شي
choices.textTransliteration: Bfaḍḍil aʕrif il-range yallī ʕindkun la-hal-waẓīfe awwal shī
choices.culturalNote: In Lebanon, salaries are almost always discussed in US dollars (fresh dollars), especially after the economic crisis. The Lebanese lira salary is considered separately.

branches.speaker: Mr. Haddad
branches.arabic: مَنْطِقي. بْنِقْدَر نِتْفاوَض عَالتَّفاصيل بَعْدين. عِنْدَك شي سُؤال إلْنا؟
branches.transliteration: Manṭiʔī. Bniʔdar nitfēwaḍ ʕat-tafēṣīl baʕdayn. ʕIndak shī suʔēl ilna?
branches.english: That's reasonable. We can negotiate the details later. Do you have any questions for us?

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose what to ask:

choices.text: Ask about work culture and team
choices.textArabic: كيف الجَوّ بِالشَّرِكة؟ وأَدَّيش عَدَد الفَريق يَلّي رَح إشْتِغِل مَعُن؟
choices.textTransliteration: Kīf ij-jaww bish-sharike? W addaysh ʕadad il-farīʔ yallī raḥ ishtighil maʕun?

choices.text: Ask about growth opportunities
choices.textArabic: في فُرَص لِلتَّطَوُّر المِهَني وتَرْقية بِالشَّرِكة؟
choices.textTransliteration: Fī furaṣ lit-taṭawwur il-mihanī w tarʔiye bish-sharike?

branches.speaker: Mr. Haddad
branches.arabic: سُؤال حِلْو. الجَوّ عِنَّا كْتير مْنيح والفَريق صْغير بَس dynamic. رَح نِتْواصَل مَعَك خِلال هَالأُسْبوع إن شاء الله.
branches.transliteration: Suʔēl ḥilw. Ij-jaww ʕinna ktīr mnīḥ wil-farīʔ zghīr bass dynamic. Raḥ nitwēṣal maʕak khilēl hal-usbūʕ in shāʔ Allāh.
branches.english: Good question. The atmosphere here is very nice and the team is small but dynamic. We will contact you during this week, God willing.
branches.culturalTip: When an interviewer says 'in shāʔ Allāh' (God willing) about next steps, it usually means they are genuinely interested but not making a firm commitment yet. It is a normal part of Lebanese communication, not evasion.

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: شُكْراً كْتير عَوَقْتَك. تْشَرَّفْت بِالمُقابْلة. يِعْطيك العافية.
branches.transliteration: Shukran ktīr ʕa-waʔtak. Tsharraft bil-muʔāble. Yaʕṭīk il-ʕēfye.
branches.english: Thank you very much for your time. I was honored by the interview. May God give you strength.

branches.speaker: Mr. Haddad
branches.arabic: الشَّرَف إلْنا. بِالتَّوفيق! نْشوفَك قَريباً إن شاء الله.
branches.transliteration: Ish-sharaf ilna. Bit-tawfīʔ! Nshūfak ʔarīban in shāʔ Allāh.
branches.english: The honor is ours. Good luck! We'll see you soon, God willing.
branches.culturalTip: Wasta (واسطة) -- personal connections -- plays a big role in Lebanese hiring. Having someone recommend you inside a company is often more important than the interview itself. However, qualifications and rapport still matter greatly.

comprehensionCheck.question: What French word is commonly used instead of 'internship' in Lebanese professional Arabic?
In what currency are salaries typically discussed in Lebanon?
What does 'tsharraft bil-muʔāble' mean?
options: I am sorry about the interview
I was honored by the interview
I want to reschedule the interview

title: At the Bank
setting: A bank branch in the Hamra district of Beirut. You need to open a new dollar account and make a transfer. The bank is busy, and you take a number and wait your turn before speaking with a teller.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: فِتِّتْ عَالبَنْك بِالحَمْرا. في ناس كْتير عَم يِسْتَنّو. أَخَدِت نِمْرة وقْعَدِت.
branches.transliteration: Fittit ʕal-bank bil-Ḥamra. Fī nēs ktīr ʕam yistannū. Akhadit nimra w ʔiʕdit.
branches.english: You enter the bank in Hamra. There are many people waiting. You take a number and sit down.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: بَعْد نُصّ ساعة، لِحِق دَوْرَك.
branches.transliteration: Baʕd nuṣṣ sēʕa, liḥiʔ dawrak.
branches.english: After half an hour, your turn comes.
branches.culturalTip: Long waits at Lebanese banks are very common, especially since the 2019 financial crisis. Patience is essential. Many people spend this time chatting with other customers.

branches.speaker: Teller
branches.arabic: أَهْلا. شو فيني ساعْدَك اليَوم؟
branches.transliteration: Ahla. Shū fīnī sēʕdak il-yōm?
branches.english: Hello. How can I help you today?

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose what you need:

choices.text: I want to open a new dollar account
choices.textArabic: بَدّي إفْتَح حِساب جْديد بِالدولار
choices.textTransliteration: Baddī iftaḥ ḥsēb jdīd bid-dolēr

choices.text: I need to make a transfer abroad
choices.textArabic: بَدّي أَعْمِل تَحْويلة عَالخارِج
choices.textTransliteration: Baddī aʕmil taḥwīle ʕal-khērij
choices.culturalNote: Transferring money abroad from Lebanon has become very complicated since the banking crisis. Many people use informal transfer services (hawala) instead of banks.

branches.speaker: Teller
branches.arabic: ماشي. لازِم يْكون مَعَك هَوِيّة أَو جَواز سَفَر، وفاتورة كَهْرَبا أَو مَي كَإثْبات عُنْوان.
branches.transliteration: Mēshī. Lēzim ykūn maʕak hawiyye aw jawēz safar, w fētūra kahraba aw mayy ka-isbēt ʕunwēn.
branches.english: Okay. You need to have an ID or passport, and an electricity or water bill as proof of address.

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: مَعي الجَواز بَس ما مَعي فاتورة. بْيِمْشي كَشْف حِساب مِن بَنْك تاني؟
branches.transliteration: Maʕī ij-jawēz bass ma maʕī fētūra. Byimshī kashf ḥsēb min bank tēnī?
branches.english: I have my passport but I don't have a bill. Would a bank statement from another bank work?

branches.speaker: Teller
branches.arabic: خَلّيني إسْأَل المُدير... إي، بْيِمْشي الحال. بَس لازِم يْكون عَليه عُنْوانَك الحالي.
branches.transliteration: Khallīnī isʔal il-mudīr... Ē, byimshī il-ḥēl. Bass lēzim ykūn ʕalayh ʕunwēnak il-ḥēlī.
branches.english: Let me ask the manager... Yes, it will work. But it must have your current address on it.
branches.culturalTip: The phrase 'byimshī il-ḥēl' literally means 'the situation walks' -- a very common Lebanese expression meaning 'it works / it is acceptable.' It reflects the flexible, case-by-case approach to rules in Lebanese culture.

branches.speaker: Teller
branches.arabic: هَلَّق خَلّيني إشْرَح الحِسابات يَلّي عِنَّا. في حِساب تَوفير بْفَوايِد وفي حِساب جاري بَلا فَوايِد. شو بْتْفَضِّل؟
branches.transliteration: Hallaʔ khallīnī ishraḥ il-ḥsēbēt yallī ʕinna. Fī ḥsēb tawfīr b-fawēyid w fī ḥsēb jērī bala fawēyid. Shū btfaḍḍil?
branches.english: Now let me explain the accounts we have. There is a savings account with interest and a checking account without interest. What do you prefer?

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose the account type:

choices.text: Savings account -- what is the interest rate?
choices.textArabic: حِساب تَوفير. قَدَّيش نِسْبة الفَوايِد؟
choices.textTransliteration: Ḥsēb tawfīr. Addaysh nisbit il-fawēyid?

choices.text: Checking account -- I need easy access to my money
choices.textArabic: حِساب جاري، بَدّي إقْدَر إسْحَب مَصاري بِأَيّ وَقْت
choices.textTransliteration: Ḥsēb jērī, baddī iʔdar isḥab maṣērī b-ayy waʔt

branches.speaker: Teller
branches.arabic: الفَوايِد عَالدولار هَلَّق حَوالي تْلاتة بِالمية سَنَوي. بَس لازِم يِبْقى الحَدّ الأَدْنى أَلْف دولار بِالحِساب.
branches.transliteration: Il-fawēyid ʕad-dolēr hallaʔ ḥawēlī tlēte bil-mīye sanawī. Bass lēzim yibʔa il-ḥadd il-adna alf dolēr bil-ḥsēb.
branches.english: Interest on dollars right now is about three percent annually. But the minimum balance of one thousand dollars must remain in the account.
branches.culturalTip: Since the Lebanese financial crisis, there is a crucial distinction between 'lollars' (old dollars trapped in the banking system, called 'dolēr bank') and 'fresh dollars' (new cash deposits). Always clarify which type of account you are dealing with.

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: وشو الرُّسوم الشَّهْرِيّة عَالحِساب؟ في رُسوم عَالسَّحْب والتَّحْويل؟
branches.transliteration: W shū ir-rusūm ish-shahriyye ʕal-ḥsēb? Fī rusūm ʕas-saḥb wal-taḥwīl?
branches.english: And what are the monthly fees on the account? Are there fees for withdrawals and transfers?

branches.speaker: Teller
branches.arabic: الرُّسوم الشَّهْرِيّة خَمْس دولارات. والسَّحْب مِن الـATM عَليه رَسْم دولار ونُصّ. التَّحْويلات المَحَلِّيّة مَجّانِيّة.
branches.transliteration: Ir-rusūm ish-shahriyye khams dolērēt. Wis-saḥb min il-ATM ʕalayh rasm dolēr w nuṣṣ. It-taḥwīlēt il-maḥalliyye majjēniyye.
branches.english: The monthly fee is five dollars. ATM withdrawals have a fee of a dollar and a half. Local transfers are free.

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose how to proceed:

choices.text: Okay, let's open the account
choices.textArabic: ماشي، يَلّا نِفْتَح الحِساب
choices.textTransliteration: Mēshī, yalla niftaḥ il-ḥsēb

choices.text: Let me think about it and come back
choices.textArabic: خَلّيني فَكِّر فيها وبِرْجَع
choices.textTransliteration: Khallīnī fakkir fīha w birjaʕ

branches.speaker: Teller
branches.arabic: تَمام. عَبّي هالفورم وأَمْضي تَحْت. بَدْنا كَمان صورة عَن الجَواز.
branches.transliteration: Tamēm. ʕAbbī hal-form w imḍī taḥt. Badna kamēn ṣūra ʕan ij-jawēz.
branches.english: Alright. Fill out this form and sign at the bottom. We also need a copy of your passport.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: عَبّيت الأَوراق وسَلَّمْتُن لِلمُوَظَّف. إنْتَظَرِت شْوَي وبَعْدين أَخَدِت رَقَم الحِساب الجْديد.
branches.transliteration: ʕAbbēt il-awrēʔ w sallamtun lil-muwaẓẓaf. Intaẓart shwayy w baʕdayn akhadit raʔm il-ḥsēb ij-jdīd.
branches.english: You fill out the papers and hand them to the employee. You wait a bit and then receive your new account number.

branches.speaker: Teller
branches.arabic: تفَضَّل، هَيدا رَقَم حِسابَك. الـcard بْتوصَل خِلال أُسْبوع عَالبيت. في شي تاني؟
branches.transliteration: Tfaḍḍal, hayda raʔm ḥsēbak. Il-card btwuṣṣal khilēl usbūʕ ʕal-bayt. Fī shī tēnī?
branches.english: Here you go, this is your account number. The card will arrive at your home within a week. Anything else?

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: لَأ، هَيدا كِلْشي. مَرْسي كْتير عَمُساعَدْتَك.
branches.transliteration: Laʔ, hayda kilshī. Mersī ktīr ʕa-musēʕadtak.
branches.english: No, that is everything. Thank you so much for your help.
branches.culturalTip: Using 'mersī' (from the French 'merci') is extremely common in Lebanese Arabic, often used interchangeably with 'shukran.' It reflects the deep French influence on everyday Lebanese speech.

branches.speaker: Teller
branches.arabic: أَهْلاً وسَهْلاً. بِالتَّوفيق! وإذا في أَيّ شي، اتَّصِل فينا.
branches.transliteration: Ahla w sahla. Bit-tawfīʔ! W iza fī ayy shī, ittaṣil fīna.
branches.english: You are welcome. Good luck! And if there is anything, call us.

comprehensionCheck.question: What does 'byimshī il-ḥēl' mean in Lebanese Arabic?
What documents does the teller require to open a bank account?
options: ID or passport and a utility bill as proof of address
A birth certificate and school diploma

comprehensionCheck.question: Why is the distinction between 'fresh dollars' and 'lollar' important in Lebanese banking?
options: Fresh dollars can be withdrawn freely while old dollars trapped in the system have restrictions
Fresh dollars can only be used online

title: Navigating Bureaucracy
setting: A government office (muʔassase) in Beirut. You need to get an official document -- a civil status extract (ikhraj ʔayd). The place is crowded, the process is confusing, and you need to navigate clerks, missing paperwork, and the famous Lebanese bureaucratic patience.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: وِصِلْتْ عَالمُؤَسَّسة الحُكومِيّة الصُّبْح بَكّير. المَحَلّ مَلْيان ناس وكِلّ واحَد ماسِك وَرَق.
branches.transliteration: Wṣilt ʕal-muʔassase il-ḥukūmiyye iṣ-ṣubiḥ bakkīr. Il-maḥall malyēn nēs w kill wēḥad mēsik waraʔ.
branches.english: You arrive at the government office early in the morning. The place is full of people and everyone is holding papers.
branches.culturalTip: Arriving early at government offices in Lebanon is crucial. Offices often have limited hours and long queues. Many people arrive before the doors even open.

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose how to find out where to go:

choices.text: Ask the security guard at the entrance
choices.textArabic: لَو سَمَحْت، وين بْروح لَإخْراج قَيْد؟ أَوَّل مَرّة جايي لَهون
choices.textTransliteration: Law samaḥt, wayn brūḥ la-ikhrēj ʔayd? Awwal marra jēyī la-hōn

choices.text: Look for an information desk
choices.textArabic: في مَكْتَب اِسْتِعْلامات هَون ولّا مين بِسْأَل؟
choices.textTransliteration: Fī maktab istiʕlēmēt hōn walla mīn bisʔal?

branches.speaker: Security Guard
branches.arabic: إخْراج قَيْد؟ الطّابِق التّاني، الشُّبّاك رَقَم أَرْبَعة. بَس خُد نِمْرة مِن عِنْد المَدْخَل أَوَّل شي.
branches.transliteration: Ikhrēj ʔayd? Iṭ-ṭēbiʔ it-tēnī, ish-shubbēk raʔm arbaʕa. Bass khud nimra min ʕind il-madkhal awwal shī.
branches.english: Civil status extract? Second floor, window number four. But take a number from the entrance first.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: طِلِعْت عَالطّابِق التّاني وقِعِدْت تِسْتَنّى. بَعْد ساعة تَقْريباً، لِحِق دَوْرَك.
branches.transliteration: Ṭliʕit ʕaṭ-ṭēbiʔ it-tēnī w ʔiʕdit tistanna. Baʕd sēʕa taʔrīban, liḥiʔ dawrak.
branches.english: You go up to the second floor and sit down to wait. After about an hour, your turn comes.
branches.culturalTip: Waiting an hour or more is completely normal in Lebanese government offices. Some people bring books or breakfast. The concept of 'bukra' (tomorrow) is deeply embedded in bureaucratic culture -- things are rarely resolved in one visit.

branches.speaker: Clerk
branches.arabic: أَهْلاً. شو بْتْريد؟
branches.transliteration: Ahlan. Shū btrīd?
branches.english: Hello. What do you need?

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: مَرْحَبا. بَدّي إخْراج قَيْد فَرْدي. هَيدي هَوِيِّتي وهَيدي صورة عَن سِجِلّ العَيْلة.
branches.transliteration: Marḥaba. Baddī ikhrēj ʔayd fardī. Haydī hawiyyitī w haydī ṣūra ʕan sijill il-ʕayle.
branches.english: Hello. I need an individual civil status extract. Here is my ID and here is a copy of the family register.

branches.speaker: Clerk
branches.arabic: خَلّيني شوف... هَيدي الهَوِيّة مْنيحة. بَس وين الطّابَع المالي؟ لازِم تْجيب طَوابِع مالِيّة مِن تَحْت.
branches.transliteration: Khallīnī shūf... Haydī il-hawiyye mnīḥa. Bass wayn iṭ-ṭēbaʕ il-mēlī? Lēzim tjīb ṭawēbiʕ mēliyye min taḥt.
branches.english: Let me see... This ID is fine. But where is the fiscal stamp? You need to bring fiscal stamps from downstairs.

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose how to respond:

choices.text: Politely ask where to get them
choices.textArabic: آه ما كِنْت عارِف. وين بْلاقي الطَّوابِع بِالزَّبْط؟ وقَدَّيش بْيْكَلّفو؟
choices.textTransliteration: Āh ma kint ʕērif. Wayn bilēʔī iṭ-ṭawēbiʕ biz-zabṭ? W addaysh byikallfu?

choices.text: Express frustration politely
choices.textArabic: بَس ما حَدا خَبَّرني عَنُّن قَبْل! ليش ما بْيْكون في لائِحة بِالأَوراق المَطْلوبة؟
choices.textTransliteration: Bass ma ḥada khabbarnī ʕannun ʔabl! Laysh ma bykūn fī lēʔiḥa bil-awrēʔ il-maṭlūbe?
choices.culturalNote: Being told you are missing a document after a long wait is a classic Lebanese bureaucratic experience. Staying calm and polite, while firm, gets you far better results than getting angry.

branches.speaker: Clerk
branches.arabic: الطَّوابِع بِالطّابِق الأَوَّل، عِنْد الشُّبّاك يَلّي عَاليَمين. رَسْمُن عَشَر دولار أَو بِاللّيرة حَسَب السِّعْر.
branches.transliteration: Iṭ-ṭawēbiʕ biṭ-ṭēbiʔ il-awwal, ʕind ish-shubbēk yallī ʕal-yamīn. Rasmun ʕashar dolēr aw bil-līra ḥasab is-siʕr.
branches.english: The stamps are on the first floor, at the window on the right. The fee is ten dollars or in lira according to the exchange rate.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: نْزِلْت وشْريت الطَّوابِع ورْجِعْت عَالطّابِق التّاني. المُوَظَّف قالَّك إرْجَع لَعِنْدو.
branches.transliteration: Nzilt w sharēt iṭ-ṭawēbiʕ w rjiʕit ʕaṭ-ṭēbiʔ it-tēnī. Il-muwaẓẓaf ʔallak irjaʕ la-ʕindo.
branches.english: You go down, buy the stamps, and come back to the second floor. The clerk tells you to come back to him.

branches.speaker: Clerk
branches.arabic: تَمام. هَلَّق عَبّي هالطَّلَب. إسْمَك الكامِل، إسْم الأَب والأُمّ، وتاريخ الميلاد.
branches.transliteration: Tamēm. Hallaʔ ʕabbī hal-ṭalab. Ismak il-kēmil, isim il-ab wil-umm, w tērīkh il-mīlēd.
branches.english: Good. Now fill out this application. Your full name, father's and mother's name, and date of birth.

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: تَمام، عَبّيتُن. إمْتا بْيْكون جاهِز الإخْراج قَيْد؟
branches.transliteration: Tamēm, ʕabbēytun. Imta bykūn jēhiz il-ikhrēj ʔayd?
branches.english: Done, I filled them out. When will the civil status extract be ready?

branches.speaker: Clerk
branches.english: The clerk gives you a timeline:

choices.text: Come back tomorrow (the classic 'bukra')
choices.textArabic: تَعا بُكْرا بَعْد الضُّهْر. بْيْكون جاهِز إن شاء الله
choices.textTransliteration: Taʕa bukra baʕd iḍ-ḍuhur. Bykūn jēhiz in shāʔ Allāh
choices.culturalNote: 'Bukra' (tomorrow) in Lebanese bureaucracy can mean literally tomorrow, or it can mean 'sometime in the near future.' The addition of 'in shāʔ Allāh' adds another layer of uncertainty.

choices.text: It will be ready in an hour
choices.textArabic: إنْطُرْني ساعة، بْخَلِّصو هَلَّق
choices.textTransliteration: Inṭurnī sēʕa, bkhallṣo hallaʔ

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose how to respond:

choices.text: Accept and ask if there is a way to speed things up
choices.textArabic: طَيِّب ماشي. في طَريقة نْسَرِّع المُعامَلة شْوَي؟
choices.textTransliteration: Ṭayyeb mēshī. Fī ṭarīʔa nsarriʕ il-muʕēmale shwayy?
choices.culturalNote: Asking to 'speed things up' can sometimes be an indirect reference to wasta or tipping. However, it can also simply be a legitimate question about expedited processing. Context and tone matter greatly.

choices.text: Accept patiently and confirm what to bring
choices.textArabic: تَمام، بُكْرا بِرْجَع. هَل لازِم جيب شي تاني مَعي؟
choices.textTransliteration: Tamēm, bukra birjaʕ. Hal lēzim jīb shī tēnī maʕī?

branches.speaker: Clerk
branches.arabic: لَأ هَيدا كِلْشي. بَس جيب مَعَك الوَصْل يَلّي رَح أَعْطيك ياه هَلَّق. بَلاه ما فينا نْسَلِّمَك شي.
branches.transliteration: Laʔ hayda kilshī. Bass jīb maʕak il-waṣl yallī raḥ aʕṭīk yēh hallaʔ. Balēh ma fīna nsallmak shī.
branches.english: No, that is everything. But bring with you the receipt I am going to give you now. Without it we cannot hand you anything.

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: تَمام، رَح إحْتِفِظ فيه. شُكْراً كْتير عَمُساعَدْتَك. يِعْطيك العافية.
branches.transliteration: Tamēm, raḥ iḥtifiẓ fīh. Shukran ktīr ʕa-musēʕadtak. Yaʕṭīk il-ʕēfye.
branches.english: Alright, I will keep it safe. Thank you very much for your help. May God give you strength.

branches.speaker: Clerk
branches.arabic: الله يْعافيك. هَيك الدَّوْلة عِنَّا -- بَدّا صَبِر! بِالتَّوفيق.
branches.transliteration: Allāh yʕēfīk. Hayk id-dawle ʕinna -- badda ṣabir! Bit-tawfīʔ.
branches.english: God bless you. That is how the government is here -- it requires patience! Good luck.
branches.culturalTip: Despite the frustrations of Lebanese bureaucracy, being polite and saying 'yaʕṭīk il-ʕēfye' to government employees goes a long way. They deal with frustrated people all day, and kindness can sometimes get you better service on your next visit.

comprehensionCheck.question: What is an 'ikhrēj ʔayd' (إخراج قيد)?
options: A civil status extract (official identity document)

comprehensionCheck.question: What did the clerk say was missing from the paperwork?
What does 'bukra' often imply in Lebanese bureaucratic contexts?
options: Sometime in the near future, not necessarily literally tomorrow
